name: Honda Fit
transmission: 6-speed manual or CVT (128 hp with the CVT)
reliability: 9/10 — the Fit is one of the most reliable small cars Honda ever built. The naturally-aspirated 1.5L has no turbo and a timing chain (no belt to change), so there's very little to go wrong; owners routinely take these past 200k miles on basic maintenance. Its NHTSA complaint count is modest and mostly comfort-related, not mechanical. The one thing to actually chase is the Takata airbag recall on older cars — free, and non-negotiable.
cost_to_own: Insurance: Low · Maintenance ~$350/yr (cheapest Honda to keep) · Excellent fuel economy · Strong resale for a subcompact — the Fit holds value because everyone knows it lasts
popular_mods: All-weather floor liners + a cargo tray — the Magic Seat makes the Fit a genuine little hauler, so protect it
Roof crossbars + bike/cargo rack (Yakima / Thule) — the Fit swallows more than cars twice its size
A second set of wheels with winter tires — the best money you can spend on a light FWD car
Mild suspension (lowering springs / sway bar) for the small but real enthusiast scene around the GK/GE
Honest take: the Fit's magic is packaging and efficiency, not power — money is better spent on good tires than on chasing horsepower it doesn't have

generations.1st Gen GD (2007–2008 US): The original clever subcompact — 1.5L (L15A, 109 hp), 5-speed manual or 5-speed auto. Tiny footprint, surprising space, and the Magic Seat debut.
generations.2nd Gen GE (2009–2013 US): More refined and a bit bigger; 1.5L (L15A7, 117 hp), 5MT or 5AT. The Sport trim added paddle shifters. Still famously durable.
generations.3rd Gen GK (2015–2020 US): The roomiest and most efficient Fit — 1.5L direct-injection L15B (130 hp / 128 with CVT), 6-speed manual or CVT. Magic Seat perfected. Honda pulled the Fit from the US after 2020 and pointed shoppers at the HR-V.
generations.Note: There was no 2014 US model year between the 2nd and 3rd gens.

common_issues: Road and wind noise — it's a light, small car, and it sounds like one on the highway
Firm, thin seats that some owners find tiring on long trips — a genuine comfort gripe, not a fault
Takata airbag recall on 2015–2016 (and the earlier generations) — verify by VIN that it's been done, it's free
Early GK infotainment (2015–2017) was laggy and dropped the physical volume knob; Honda brought the knob back in the 2018 refresh
The CVT is efficient but drones under hard acceleration — the 6-speed manual is the enthusiast's pick
maintenance_tips: Keep up with 0W-20 oil on the Maintenance Minder — this engine asks for almost nothing else
Service the CVT fluid with genuine Honda HCF-2 around 30–40k miles — the CVT lasts when you actually service it
Check the Takata airbag and any open recalls by VIN before you buy an older one
Beyond that it's cabin filters, brake fluid, and tires — parts are cheap and everywhere
The manual's clutch is light and long-lived; just do the fluid and it'll outlast the car
